sentence: Subjective:

The patient, a 60-year-old male, presents with a 3-4 month history of difficulty adjusting to dark environments, specifically when reading in dimly lit places, and difficulty perceiving straight lines as wavy.
He reports that this has been worsening over the past few months.
The patient also mentions that he has trouble with his vision when looking at his office paneling, which has dark grooves that appear wavy to him.
He denies any fever or other symptoms.

The patient and his wife frequently dine out and recently visited a restaurant, which he described as "phenomenal." He reports a history of smoking a pack a day, despite previous attempts to quit through hypnosis, lozenges, and chewing gum.
The patient has a history of high blood pressure and admits to not taking his blood pressure medication as prescribed.
Past medical history:
The patient has a history of high blood pressure and is a smoker.
He has tried to quit smoking through various methods but has been unsuccessful.
Review of systems:
The patient denies any other symptoms.
Current medications:
The patient is not currently taking his blood pressure medication as prescribed.
Objective:
Vital signs:
Blood pressure: [NOT MENTIONED]
Pulse rate: [NOT MENTIONED]
Oxygen saturation: [NOT MENTIONED]
Temperature: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam:
The anterior segment examination reveals posterior chamber intraocular lenses in both eyes.
The cornea is clear in both eyes.
The peak hole cup to disc ratio is 0.2 in the right eye and 0.3 in the left eye.
The fundus examination of the right eye shows the retina to be attached 360 degrees with no holes or tears noted.
RPE changes are present in the fovea.
The posterior segment exam shows drusen in the macula, retinal thickening, and presence of subretinal fluid and exudate accumulation, and a hemorrhage in the right eye.
The cornea is clear in the left eye, and the retina is attached appropriately with no hemorrhage or subretinal fluid noted.
Test Results:
The OCT exam shows drusen deposits under the RPE, subretinal fluid accumulation, and choroidal neovascular membrane.
The fluorescein angiography reveals a focal area of leakage in the right eye consistent with neovascular age-related macular degeneration.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has neovascular age-related macular degeneration, also known as wet macular degeneration, which is an advanced stage that typically leads to more severe vision loss.
Problem list: Age-related macular degeneration, suspected; high blood pressure, not controlled; smoking, active.
Plan:
I prescribed Lucentis injections to be administered once a month to slow the progression of the disease.
The patient will receive the first injection today.
I will also instruct the patient to take his blood pressure medication as prescribed and to check his blood pressures on a regular basis.
I will have the nurse prepare the patient for the injection.
Follow-up:
I will schedule a follow-up appointment to monitor the patient's response to the treatment and to administer subsequent injections as needed.
